section.title: The Oracle against Babylon
section.sectionRationale: This movement opens the great block of oracles against the nations running through chapters 13-23, beginning with Babylon, the empire that would one day destroy Jerusalem itself. It pairs a cosmic vision of the Day of the LORD unraveling creation's lights with a taunting funeral song over the fallen king of Babylon, whose prideful ascent to heaven becomes the pattern later applied to Satan's own fall.
section.thesis: The LORD who compassionately restores Jacob will also, with the same sovereign hand, summon the Medes to bring the terrible Day of the LORD against proud Babylon, humbling the very king who dreamed of ascending above the stars of God down to the depths of Sheol.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Day of the LORD as cosmic upheaval; the summoned army and Babylon's coming desolation; the taunt song over the fallen king of Babylon; and the certainty of the LORD's purposed plan for the whole earth.

units.label: The Day of the LORD
units.note: the stars will not give their light

verses.text: This is the burden against Babylon that Isaiah son of Amoz received:
Raise a banner on a barren hilltop; call aloud to them. Wave your hand, that they may enter the gates of the nobles.
I have commanded My sanctified ones; I have even summoned My warriors to execute My wrath and exult in My triumph.
Listen, a tumult on the mountains, like that of a great multitude! Listen, an uproar among the kingdoms, like nations gathered together! The LORD of Hosts is mobilizing an army for war.
They are coming from faraway lands, from the ends of the heavens, the LORD and the weapons of His wrath, to destroy the whole country.
Wail, for the {{yom_yhwh2|Day of the LORD}} is near; it will come as destruction from the Almighty.
Therefore all hands will fall limp, and every man's heart will melt.
Terror, pain, and anguish will seize them; they will writhe like a woman in labor. They will look at one another, their faces flushed with fear.
Behold, the Day of the LORD is coming, cruel, with fury and burning anger, to make the earth a desolation and to destroy the sinners within it.
For the stars of heaven and their constellations will not give their light. The rising sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light.
I will punish the world for its evil and the wicked for their iniquity. I will end the haughtiness of the arrogant and lay low the pride of the ruthless.
I will make man scarcer than pure gold, and mankind rarer than the gold of Ophir.
Therefore I will make the heavens tremble, and the earth will be shaken from its place at the wrath of the LORD of Hosts on the day of His burning anger.

units.label: Babylon's desolation
units.note: the Medes summoned, Babylon like Sodom

verses.text: Like a hunted gazelle, like a sheep without a shepherd, each will return to his own people, each will flee to his native land.
Whoever is caught will be stabbed, and whoever is captured will die by the sword.
Their infants will be dashed to pieces before their eyes, their houses will be looted, and their wives will be ravished.

Behold, I will stir up against them the Medes, who have no regard for silver 
and no desire for gold.

Their bows will dash young men to pieces; they will have no mercy on the fruit of the womb; 
they will not look with pity on the children.
And Babylon, the jewel of the kingdoms, the glory of the pride of the Chaldeans, will be overthrown by God like Sodom and Gomorrah.
She will never be inhabited or settled from generation to generation; no nomad will pitch his tent there, no shepherd will rest his flock there.
But desert creatures will lie down there, and howling creatures will fill her houses. Ostriches will dwell there, and wild goats will leap about.
Hyenas will howl in her fortresses and jackals in her luxurious palaces. Babylon's time is at hand, and her days will not be prolonged.
For the LORD will have compassion on Jacob; once again He will choose Israel and settle them in their own land. The foreigner will join them and unite with the house of Jacob.

The nations will escort Israel and bring it to its homeland. 
Then the house of Israel will possess the nations as menservants and maidservants in the LORD's land. They will make captives of their captors and rule over their oppressors.
On the day that the LORD gives you rest from your pain and torment, and from the hard labor into which you were forced,

units.label: The taunt over the fallen king
units.note: how you have fallen from heaven, O day star

verses.text:
you will sing this song of contempt against the king of Babylon: 
How the oppressor has ceased, and how his fury has ended!
The LORD has broken the staff of the wicked, the scepter of the rulers.
It struck the peoples in anger with unceasing blows; it subdued the nations in rage with relentless persecution.
All the earth is at peace and at rest; they break out in song.
Even the cypresses and cedars of Lebanon exult over you: “Since you have been laid low, no woodcutter comes against us.”
Sheol beneath is eager to meet you upon your arrival. It stirs the spirits of the dead to greet you, all the rulers of the earth. It makes all the kings of the nations rise from their thrones.

They will all respond to you, saying, “You too have become weak, as we are; 
you have become like us!”
Your pomp has been brought down to Sheol, along with the music of your harps. Maggots are your bed and worms your blanket.
How you have fallen from heaven, O {{heylel|day star}}, son of the dawn! You have been cut down to the ground, O destroyer of nations.
You said in your heart: “I will ascend to the heavens; I will raise my throne above the stars of God. I will sit on the mount of assembly, in the far reaches of the north.
I will ascend above the tops of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High.”
But you will be brought down to Sheol, to the lowest depths of the Pit.
Those who see you will stare; they will ponder your fate: “Is this the man who shook the earth and made the kingdoms tremble,
who turned the world into a desert and destroyed its cities, who refused to let the captives return to their homes?”
All the kings of the nations lie in state, each in his own tomb.
But you are cast out of your grave like a rejected branch, covered by those slain with the sword, and dumped into a rocky pit like a carcass trampled underfoot.

You will not join them in burial, since you have destroyed your land 
and slaughtered your own people. The offspring of the wicked will never again be mentioned.
Prepare a place to slaughter his sons for the iniquities of their forefathers. They will never rise up to possess a land or cover the earth with their cities.

“I will rise up against them,” declares the LORD of Hosts. “I will cut off from Babylon her name and her remnant, 
her offspring and her posterity,” declares the LORD.

“I will make her a place for owls and for swamplands; I will sweep her away with the broom of destruction,” 
declares the LORD of Hosts.

yom_yhwh2.gloss: The Day of the LORD, a decisive moment of divine intervention. Here it describes cosmic-scale judgment on Babylon using apocalyptic imagery, stars withholding light, heavens trembling, language later echoed in the New Testament's descriptions of final, universal judgment, showing how a specific historical judgment can carry echoes of the ultimate one.

heylel.gloss: Day star, son of the dawn, translated in the Latin Vulgate as Lucifer, light-bearer. Though addressed directly to the historical king of Babylon's prideful fall, the language of ascending to heaven and being cast down has long been read by Christian interpreters as also describing the primal fall of Satan, whose pride mirrors this king's own.

basic.context: This oracle opens a long series of judgments against the nations, beginning fittingly with Babylon, the empire that would eventually destroy Jerusalem and take Judah into exile. Isaiah describes the Day of the LORD in cosmic terms, darkened stars, a trembling earth, before naming the Medes as the specific instrument of Babylon's fall. The passage climaxes in a biting funeral taunt sung over Babylon's fallen king, whose ambition to ascend above the stars of God is instead brought down to the depths of Sheol.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a genuine, historically anchored prophecy against a real empire, later fulfilled when the Medo-Persian empire conquered Babylon in 539 BC, though written more than a century before Babylon even rose to be Judah's chief threat, showing remarkable prophetic reach. The most debated question is whether the king of Babylon taunt in 14:12-15 describes only that human king or also depicts, in its language of prideful ascent and cosmic fall, the deeper spiritual reality of Satan's own primal rebellion; church tradition has long read both together without needing to deny either.
basic.originalLanguages: The passage describes the yom YHWH, Day of the LORD, in cosmic terms that stretch beyond any single historical event into apocalyptic language later echoed for the final judgment. The fallen king is addressed as heylel ben shachar, day star, son of the dawn, rendered Lucifer in the Latin Vulgate, language whose ambition to ascend above the stars of God has long been read as reflecting the deeper pattern of Satan's own original fall from heaven.
basic.typology: The king of Babylon's prideful ascent and catastrophic fall becomes the paradigmatic Old Testament picture of the fall of any proud, self-exalting power, later applied by Jesus Himself, Luke 10:18, to Satan's fall, and taken up throughout Christian tradition as describing the primal rebellion behind every subsequent human tyranny. The Day of the LORD's cosmic imagery, darkened sun, moon, and stars, anticipates and previews the final Day of Christ's return, described in remarkably similar terms in Matthew 24:29.
basic.christ: Jesus directly echoes this passage's imagery when He tells His disciples, I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven, Luke 10:18, applying language remarkably close to Isaiah's day star cast down from heaven to the ultimate spiritual power behind every proud human empire. Where Babylon's king sought to make himself like the Most High through self-exaltation, Christ, who truly is equal with God, took the opposite path, humbling Himself even to death on a cross, Philippians 2:6-8, and it is precisely this humility that God exalts, in direct contrast to Babylon's pride that God brings down.
basic.god: God is sovereign not merely over Israel's own history but over the rise and fall of every empire, using the Medes as deliberately as He used Assyria, showing His purposes span the entire earth. He opposes pride absolutely, wherever it appears, whether in a human king who dreams of ascending to heaven or in the deeper spiritual rebellion such pride echoes, and He alone determines which powers rise and how far they fall.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 25:1-21 and Daniel 5 narrate the historical outworking of Babylon's rise and eventual fall to the Medo-Persians. Luke 10:18 has Jesus directly describe Satan's fall in language echoing this very passage. Revelation 18 develops an extended, later oracle against 'Babylon' as a symbol for all worldly, God-opposing power, drawing heavily on this chapter's imagery. Philippians 2:6-11 contrasts Babylon's self-exalting pride with Christ's self-emptying humility that God exalts instead.
basic.oneStory: This oracle shows that God's sovereignty extends over every empire and every proud heart that exalts itself against Him, whether a historical king of Babylon or the deeper spiritual rebellion his pride reflects. The whole story moves toward the ultimate Day of the LORD, when every proud power will finally be brought low, and only the truly humbled and exalted King, Christ, will remain standing.

context.literary: The unit combines an oracle of cosmic Day-of-the-LORD judgment (13:1-13), a specific announcement of Babylon's historical desolation by the Medes (13:14-14:3), and a mashal, a taunt-song or proverb of derision, sung over the fallen king (14:4-21), closing with a final divine oath (14:22-23). This taunt-song form, addressing a defeated enemy in mocking poetic speech, is a recognized ancient Near Eastern genre also seen in Ezekiel's oracles against Tyre and Egypt.
context.historical: Remarkably, this oracle was likely composed in the eighth century BC, when Babylon was not yet Judah's primary threat, Assyria was, yet it precisely anticipates Babylon's later rise to imperial dominance and its eventual fall to the Medo-Persian empire under Cyrus in 539 BC, exactly as verse 17 specifically names the Medes as the instrument of conquest, centuries before that alliance became historically decisive.
context.canonical: The taunt against the king of Babylon in 14:12-15 became one of the most influential texts in the history of Christian theology regarding the origin and nature of Satan's fall, its imagery of prideful ascent and catastrophic descent shaping centuries of reflection on the nature of evil, pride, and rebellion against God, and directly echoed in Revelation's later, expanded oracles against a symbolic 'Babylon.'

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce, more than a century in advance, the eventual rise and certain fall of the empire that would one day destroy Jerusalem, and to expose the ultimate futility and destiny of any power, human or spiritual, that exalts itself in pride against God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical judgment on a specific ancient empire and its ruler; it is not a prescription for human warfare or vengeance against modern political rivals. The enduring principle is that God actively opposes pride and self-exaltation wherever it appears and that no empire, however glorious, stands beyond His sovereign judgment.

debates.question: Does Isaiah 14:12-15, the taunt against the day star, describe only the historical king of Babylon, or does it also depict Satan's primal fall?

views.label: Primarily and directly about the historical king of Babylon
views.case: The passage explicitly introduces itself as a taunt-song 'against the king of Babylon,' 14:4, using standard ancient Near Eastern royal and mythological imagery of divine kingship and cosmic ambition to mock a specific, defeated human tyrant, without necessarily intending a separate reference to a heavenly being.

views.label: A description of the historical king that also echoes or points to Satan's fall
views.case: The language of ascending above the stars of God and being cast down to Sheol goes beyond what fits a merely human king, and centuries of Christian interpretation, along with Jesus's own statement in Luke 10:18 about Satan falling like lightning, have read this passage as reflecting the deeper spiritual pattern of pride and fall behind every proud human tyranny, this king included.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together: the primary, grammatical referent is the historical king of Babylon, mocked in a specific ancient taunt-song, while the extremity of the imagery has rightly led interpreters throughout history to see in it the pattern, and perhaps the ultimate source, of the pride that animates every such tyrant, culminating in Satan's own primal rebellion against God.

study.originalLanguages: The oracle describes the yom YHWH, Day of the LORD, in cosmic terms, darkened stars, a trembling earth, language that stretches this specific historical judgment toward the apocalyptic imagery later used of the final, universal Day of Christ's return. The fallen king is mocked as heylel ben shachar, day star, son of the dawn, rendered Lucifer, light-bearer, in the Latin Vulgate, whose boast to ascend above the stars of God and be like the Most High has for centuries been read as echoing the deeper, original pattern of Satan's own prideful fall from heaven.

typology.type: The king of Babylon's prideful ascent and catastrophic fall, Isaiah 14:12-15
typology.fulfillment: Jesus's own description of Satan's fall, I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven, Luke 10:18.

typology.type: The Day of the LORD's cosmic upheaval, darkened sun, moon, and stars, Isaiah 13:9-10
typology.fulfillment: The final, universal Day of Christ's return, described in remarkably similar cosmic terms in Matthew 24:29.

study.christ: Jesus directly echoes this passage's imagery when He tells His disciples, I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven, Luke 10:18, applying language remarkably close to Isaiah's day star cast down from heaven to the ultimate spiritual power behind every proud human empire, including Babylon's own king. Where this king sought self-exaltation, to make myself like the Most High, Christ took the opposite path, though truly equal with God, humbling Himself to the point of death on a cross, and it is precisely this humility, not self-exalting pride, that God exalts to the highest place, Philippians 2:6-11.
study.god: God is sovereign not merely over Israel's own history but over the rise and fall of every empire on earth, deliberately raising up the Medes against Babylon just as He had earlier raised up Assyria, proof His purposes span the entire earth, not one nation alone. He opposes pride absolutely, wherever it appears, in a human king who dreams of ascending to heaven or in the deeper spiritual rebellion such pride ultimately echoes, and He alone determines which powers rise and how far, and how completely, they eventually fall.

crossReferences.note: Narrates the historical night Babylon fell to the Medo-Persians, the fulfillment of this oracle's promised judgment.
Jesus directly describes Satan's fall in language closely echoing this passage's fallen day star.
Develops an extended, later oracle against 'Babylon' as a symbol for all worldly, God-opposing power, drawing heavily on this chapter's imagery.
Contrasts Babylon's self-exalting pride with Christ's self-emptying humility, which God exalts instead.

study.oneStory: This oracle shows that God's sovereignty extends over every empire and every proud heart that exalts itself against Him, whether the historical king of Babylon or the deeper spiritual rebellion his pride reflects. The whole biblical story moves toward the ultimate Day of the LORD, when every proud power will finally be brought low, and only the truly humbled and exalted King, Christ, will remain standing forever.

quiz.q: What does the cosmic language of stars withholding their light and the earth trembling in 13:9-13 signal?

options.text: The Day of the LORD is being described in terms so severe they stretch beyond a single historical event toward ultimate, final judgment.
options.note: This apocalyptic imagery is later echoed for the final, universal Day of Christ's return in Matthew 24:29.

options.text: A literal, permanent end to the sun, moon, and stars in the eighth century BC.
options.note: The immediate historical referent is Babylon's fall; the cosmic language functions symbolically to convey the severity of judgment.

options.text: A minor local weather event affecting only Babylon's harvest.
options.note: The scale of the language, heavens trembling, earth shaken from its place, far exceeds a mere weather event.

options.text: A description exclusively of Israel's own coming judgment, not Babylon's.
options.note: The oracle is explicitly introduced as 'the burden against Babylon,' 13:1.

quiz.q: Who does the oracle specifically name as the instrument of Babylon's fall in 13:17?

options.text: The Medes.
options.note: This was written well over a century before the Medo-Persian conquest of Babylon in 539 BC actually occurred.

options.text: The Egyptians.
options.note: Egypt is not named as Babylon's conqueror in this passage; the Medes are named specifically.

options.text: The Philistines.
options.note: The Philistines are addressed in a separate later oracle, not named here as Babylon's conqueror.

options.text: The Assyrians.
options.note: Assyria is the power God used against Israel and Judah earlier in Isaiah, but here the Medes specifically are named against Babylon.

quiz.q: What does the king of Babylon boast in his heart, according to 14:13-14?

options.text: That he would ascend to the heavens and make himself like the Most High.
options.note: This self-exalting ambition is precisely what invites his catastrophic fall to Sheol.

options.text: That he would build a new temple for the LORD.
options.note: His stated ambition is self-exaltation above God, the opposite of honoring the LORD.

options.text: That he would peacefully retire from his throne.
options.note: His boast is one of grasping for supreme, even divine, status, not stepping down.

options.text: That he would show mercy to all conquered peoples.
options.note: The passage describes him as a destroyer of nations who showed no such mercy.

quiz.q: How does Jesus's statement in Luke 10:18, 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven,' relate to this passage?

options.text: It echoes the imagery of the fallen day star cast down from heaven, applying the pattern of prideful ascent and fall to Satan.
options.note: This connection has shaped centuries of Christian reflection on the nature of Satan's original rebellion.

options.text: It has no relationship to Isaiah 14 whatsoever.
options.note: The imagery and language are strikingly close, and this connection is widely recognized in Christian interpretation.

options.text: It proves Isaiah 14 refers exclusively and only to Satan, with no reference to the king of Babylon.
options.note: The text itself explicitly names the king of Babylon as its primary subject in 14:4; both readings are typically held together.

options.text: It describes a future event still awaiting fulfillment.
options.note: Jesus speaks of this fall as something He has already seen, using the past tense.

quiz.q: What contrast does Philippians 2:6-11 draw with the pattern seen in the king of Babylon's downfall?

options.text: Christ, though truly equal with God, humbled Himself, and it is that humility, not self-exaltation, that God exalts.
options.note: This is the exact reverse pattern of Babylon's king, who sought self-exaltation and was instead brought down.

options.text: Christ also sought to ascend above the stars of God through self-promotion.
options.note: Philippians 2 describes Christ's self-emptying humility, the opposite of self-exalting ambition.

options.text: There is no meaningful contrast between the two passages.
options.note: The contrast is deliberate and theologically significant: pride brought low versus humility exalted.

options.text: Both figures were exalted for the same reasons.
options.note: The reasons are opposite: Babylon's king sought self-exaltation and fell, while Christ humbled Himself and was exalted by God.

questions: This oracle shows that no empire, however glorious, stands beyond God's judgment. What 'Babylon,' a source of security or pride in your own life, might God be calling you to hold more loosely?

The king of Babylon's downfall came from wanting to ascend and be like the Most High. Where in your own heart do you notice similar impulses toward self-exaltation rather than humble dependence on God?

Christ took the opposite path from Babylon's king, humbling Himself rather than grasping for status, and God exalted Him because of it. Where is God inviting you to choose humility over self-promotion this week?

The Day of the LORD is described as certain and overwhelming, yet God also promised compassion and rest for His own people in the middle of this oracle. How do you hold together reverent fear of God's justice with confidence in His compassion toward you?

This prophecy against Babylon was given more than a century before Babylon even became Judah's chief threat. How does God's precise, long-range faithfulness in this oracle encourage you to trust promises He has made that have not yet been fulfilled in your own life?

translationNotes.note: 'Burden,' massa, is a technical term introducing a weighty, often judgment-oriented prophetic oracle, used repeatedly to head each of the oracles against the nations in chapters 13-23.
'Song of contempt,' mashal, is a term for a proverb, taunt, or satirical poem, a recognized genre for mocking a defeated enemy, seen also in later oracles against Tyre and Egypt in Ezekiel.

'Day star, son of the dawn,' heylel ben shachar, was translated Lucifer, 'light-bearer,' in the Latin Vulgate, a rendering that has profoundly shaped later Christian and popular usage of 'Lucifer' as a name for Satan, though the immediate Hebrew text addresses the king of Babylon directly.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 13:1-14:23.
